1: In the spring — when kings go out to war — Joab leads Israel to ravage the Ammonites while David remains in Jerusalem. The Chronicler condenses the entire Bathsheba episode (2 Sam 11–12) into a narrative gap. By omitting David’s gravest sin, the Chronicler presents the idealized David as a type of the coming king — not denying historical reality but focusing his lens on the Davidic covenant. Christ fulfills what David’s type only pointed toward: a king without moral failure, the only one whose perfect reign can serve as the eternal temple of God’s presence among his people.
2: David takes the crown of Milcom (the Ammonite god) from his head — its gold and precious stones placed on David’s own head. The transfer of a defeated god’s crown to the victorious king is a theological statement: YHWH’s anointed king is greater than the false gods of the nations. Revelation depicts Christ as the one bearing “many crowns” (Revelation 19:12) — the accumulated glory of all defeated false claimants to sovereignty transferred to the one true King. Every crown the nations offered their idols belongs by right to Christ.
3: David assigns the Ammonite population to work with saws, iron picks, and axes — forced labor in the service of the Davidic kingdom. The subjugation of the defeated is the ancient vocabulary for the subjugation of all creation to the dominion of the image-bearer (Genesis 1:28; Psalms 8:6). Paul reads this Adamic dominion as fulfilled in Christ: “God has put all things in subjection under his feet” (1 Corinthians 15:27; Ephesians 1:22; Hebrews 2:8) — including the “last enemy,” death itself.
4: Sibbecai the Hushathite strikes down Sippai, a descendant of the Rephaim — the race of giants. The campaign against the giants of Gath (vv4-8) itemizes the systematic elimination of every remaining giant the Philistines could field. In Israelite tradition the Rephaim/Nephilim represent the primordial threat to God’s human image-bearers — the “mighty men who were of old, the men of renown” (Genesis 6:4). The Davidic warriors who eliminate them one by one are types of Christ’s definitive defeat of “the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil” (Hebrews 2:14).
5: Elhanan son of Jair strikes down Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite — harmonizing the apparent contradiction with 2 Samuel 21:19 where Elhanan kills Goliath himself (the Chronicler adds “the brother of”). The Davidic warriors continue the work David began at the Valley of Elah: the giant Goliath who dominated and paralyzed all Israel is overcome — and now his family line is systematically defeated. As Christ defeated the accuser at the cross and now his people continue to overcome him “by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony” (Revelation 12:11), the Davidic warriors’ campaign extends their king’s initial victory.
6: A man of great stature — six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot: twenty-four digits — defies Israel. The giant’s physical abnormality marks him as belonging to a different order of humanity, the corrupted line that must be eliminated for the image of God to be restored. The number 24 is associated in Revelation with the 24 elders around the throne (Revelation 4:4) — the perfected community of God’s redeemed. The abnormal 24 of the giant’s digits is a dark mirror of the redeemed 24; Christ’s victory over the corrupted order makes way for the restored order of the new creation.
7: Jonathan son of Shimea, David’s brother, strikes him down. The elimination of the last giant — by a member of David’s own family — signals the completion of David’s conquest program. The final enemy overcome by a member of the king’s household mirrors the eschatological defeat of death: “the last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Corinthians 15:26), and it is destroyed not by a human warrior but by the Son of Man who entered death and emerged victorious (Revelation 1:18: “I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades”).
8: “These were descended from the giants in Gath, and they fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.” The summary verdict — the entire giant opposition eliminated “by the hand of David and his servants” — is the OT template for 1 Corinthians 15:57: “Thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.” The victory belongs to the king; his servants participate in it. The church does not achieve victory independently but shares in the victory Christ has won — “more than conquerors through him who loved us” (Romans 8:37).
